test_description: For each masked temperature map, fit and subtract the monopole and dipole on the unmasked pixels, then compute pseudo-a_lm up to l=5. For each l=2..5, form the normalized angular-momentum dispersion tensor T_ij^(l) = [sum_mm' a_lm^* ((L_i L_j + L_j L_i)/2)_mm' a_lm'] / [l(l+1) sum_m |a_lm|^2], where L_i are the spin-l angular momentum matrices. Sum these tensors over l=2..5, find the unit eigenvector n_max with the largest eigenvalue, and return the single scalar A = |n_max · n_ecl|, where n_ecl is the ecliptic north-pole direction in the map coordinate system. Large A indicates an unusually strong alignment of the combined low-l preferred axis with the ecliptic pole.
test_hypothesis: The Planck CMB map has an anomalously strong alignment between the combined low-\(\ell\) power-tensor preferred axis for \(\ell=2..5\) and the ecliptic pole, i.e. \(A=|n_{\max}\cdot n_{\rm ecl}|\) is unusually large compared with simulations.
justificaton: The statistic is constructed so that larger values of \(A\) mean closer alignment with the ecliptic pole, and the test description/code explicitly identify large \(A\) as the anomalous direction of interest.

custom_summary.test_description: Low-l Power-Tensor Ecliptic Alignment: for a masked temperature map, fit and subtract the monopole and dipole on unmasked pixels, compute masked pseudo-a_lm through ell=5, form and sum normalized angular-momentum dispersion tensors for ell=2..5, and return A=|n_max dot n_ecl| for the ecliptic north pole (Galactic map coordinates assumed).
custom_summary.statistic: A = |n_max . n_ecl|, where n_max is the largest-eigenvalue axis of sum_{ell=2}^5 T_ij^(ell)
custom_summary.tail_of_interest: upper tail; larger A means the combined low-ell power-tensor axis is closer to the ecliptic north pole
custom_summary.ecliptic_north_assumption: n_ecl is the J2000 ecliptic north pole expressed in standard Galactic HEALPix map coordinates (l=96.384 deg, b=29.812 deg)

plot_spec.title: Low-ell power-tensor alignment with the ecliptic pole
plot_spec.caption: Distribution of the alignment of the summed ell=2..5 angular-momentum-dispersion preferred axis with the ecliptic north pole. Values near 1 indicate unusually close alignment.

custom_summary.interpretation: The Planck alignment is not in the highest 5% tail of the simulation distribution for this statistic.

test_summary.Description: Monopole/dipole-subtracted masked Planck temperature maps were reduced to pseudo-\(a_{\ell m}\) for \(\ell\le5\). Normalized angular-momentum-dispersion tensors for \(\ell=2..5\) were summed, and the largest-eigenvalue axis was compared with the ecliptic north pole using \(A=|n_{\max}\cdot n_{\rm ecl}|\).
test_summary.Results: Planck gives \(A=0.0107\), corresponding to an angle of \(89.39^\circ\) from the ecliptic pole. Simulations have mean \(A=0.507\), standard deviation 0.287, and median 0.515. For the pre-specified upper-tail test, \(p=0.993\) with 1000 simulations; only 7 simulations had smaller \(A\), giving an opposite-tail/two-sided extremeness of about 0.016.
test_summary.Interpretation: The Planck axis is almost orthogonal to the ecliptic pole, not unusually aligned with it. Thus the stated upper-tail hypothesis is not supported, although the opposite, near-ecliptic-plane orientation would be unusual under a two-sided or lower-tail post-hoc framing.
test_summary.Comparison to literature: Related low-\(\ell\) axis and ecliptic-plane anomalies are well documented, including multipole-vector and angular-momentum-axis studies reporting quadrupole-octopole/ecliptic associations at roughly \(\gtrsim2\sigma\) (Weeks 2004, arXiv:astro-ph/0412231; Abramo et al. 2006, arXiv:astro-ph/0604346; Planck Collaboration 2015 isotropy analysis, arXiv:1506.07135). This exact summed \(\ell=2..5\) power-tensor pole-alignment statistic is not the standard published test.
